questions.text: When was the tobacco mosaic virus successfully crystallized?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: It is a fact that the tobacco mosaic virus was successfully crystallized and purified in 1935.
Tobacco mosaic virus was successfully crystallized and purified in 1935. This option is incorrect.
Tobacco mosaic virus was successfully crystallized and purified in 1935. This option is incorrect.
Tobacco mosaic virus was successfully crystallized and purified in 1935. This option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: The tobacco mosaic virus (TMV) was first successfully crystallized in 1935 by the American biochemist Wendell Meredith Stanley.This work was groundbreaking because it showed that viruses could be isolated and crystallized like chemicals, which challenged the prevailing view of viruses as "living organisms" and helped establish virology as a scientific field. Stanley was awarded the 1946 Nobel Prize in Chemistry for this achievement.

questions.text: Where does the bacteriophage replicate?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: As the name says, it infects bacteria and not human beings.
As the name says, it infects bacteria and not horses.
As the name says, it infects bacteria. It attacks bacterial cells and uses their biosynthetic machinery to replicate.
As the name says, it infects bacteria and not animals.

questions.explanation: Bacteriophages replicate inside bacterial cells.They attach to the bacterium, inject their genetic material, use the host’s machinery to make new phage DNA and proteins, assemble new viruses, and then release them (often by lysing the bacterial cell).

questions.text: Which of the following causes mad cow disease?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Prions are abnormal proteins that can cause other proteins in the brain to misfold, leading to neurodegenerative diseases such as mad cow disease. These misfolded proteins accumulate in the brain and cause damage.
Viruses are infectious agents that can cause a wide range of diseases in animals and humans. However, Mad Cow Disease is not caused by a virus.
Bacteria are single-celled microorganisms that can cause various infections and diseases. However, Mad Cow Disease is not caused by bacteria. It is primarily associated with prions.
This option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: Mad Cow Disease (Bovine Spongiform Encephalopathy, BSE) is a fatal neurodegenerative disorder in cattle caused by prions—misfolded proteins that damage the brain. It spreads mainly through contaminated feed containing infected nervous tissue. In cattle, symptoms include loss of coordination, behavioral changes, difficulty walking, weight loss, and death. In humans (variant Creutzfeldt–Jakob disease), it causes memory loss, confusion, personality changes, muscle stiffness, and coordination problems, eventually leading to coma and death. Prevention relies on banning contaminated animal feed, strict meat inspection, and culling infected animals.

questions.text: Influenza is caused by:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Adenovirus is a group of viruses that can cause a variety of respiratory and eye infections.
Poxvirus is a group of viruses that can cause a variety of diseases, including smallpox, chickenpox, and monkeypox.
Influenza is caused by the influenza virus. Influenza viruses are a group of viruses that can cause respiratory illness.
Herpes virus is a group of viruses that can cause a variety of diseases, including cold sores, genital herpes, and shingles.

questions.explanation: Influenza is caused specifically by the influenza virus because it belongs to the Orthomyxoviridae family, which has unique properties — it is an enveloped, negative-sense RNA virus with a segmented genome that determines the typical flu symptoms (fever, cough, sore throat, body aches). Other viruses you mentioned — herpesviruses (DNA viruses), variola virus causing smallpox (Poxviridae, DNA virus), and adenoviruses (DNA viruses) — belong to completely different viral families with distinct structures, replication strategies, and disease profiles. They cause illnesses like cold sores, chickenpox/shingles (herpes family), smallpox (variola), or respiratory and eye infections (adenovirus), but not influenza. In short, disease causation is tied to the specific virus family and its biology, so only influenza viruses can cause flu.

questions.text: All of the following are the current preventive methods of HIV infection, except
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Practicing safe and protected lifestyle choices, such as using condoms during sexual intercourse, engaging in monogamous relationships, and avoiding sharing needles or other drug paraphernalia, can significantly reduce the risk of HIV transmission. Therefore, this statement is correct.

options.text: Use of sterile injections and needles.
options.explanation: Using sterile injections and needles is an important preventive measure to reduce the risk of HIV transmission, particularly among people who inject drugs. Sharing needles can lead to the exchange of blood and potential transmission of HIV. Therefore, this statement is correct.

There is currently no vaccine available to prevent HIV infection. Researchers and scientists have been working on developing an HIV vaccine, but to date, no effective vaccine has been approved for use. Therefore, this statement is incorrect.

Safe blood transfusion methods, including proper screening of donated blood for HIV and other infectious diseases, help prevent the transmission of HIV through blood transfusions. Testing donated blood for HIV ensures that only safe blood is used for transfusions. Therefore, this statement is correct.

questions.explanation: HIV (human immunodeficiency virus) is a virus that attacks the body's immune system. If HIV is not treated, it can lead to AIDS (acquired immunodeficiency syndrome). There is currently no effective cure; hence, there are no available vaccines for it.

questions.text: Which of the following is not a component of HIV?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: HIV is a retrovirus, which means that it has RNA as its genetic material.
Ribosomes are not a component of HIV. HIV is a retrovirus, which means that it has RNA as its genetic material. Ribosomes are not found in viruses, as they are only found in cells.
The envelope is the outer layer of the virus that protects it from the environment. HIV is an enveloped virus.
The matrix is the layer between the envelope and the RNA.

questions.explanation: Ribosomes are not a component of HIV. HIV is a retrovirus, which means that it has RNA as its genetic material. Ribosomes are not found in viruses, as they are only found in cells.

questions.text: Which of the following is not a viral disease?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: AIDS is a viral illness that develops when a person is infected with the human immunodeficiency virus, or HIV. This virus weakens the immune system over time, making the body more vulnerable to infections and certain cancers.
Malaria is not a viral illness. It’s caused by protozoa of the genus Plasmodium, which are transmitted to humans through the bite of infected female Anopheles mosquitoes.
Influenza is a viral infection, and it’s caused by the influenza virus, which mainly affects the respiratory tract.
Chickenpox is a viral infection caused by the varicella-zoster virus. It typically leads to an itchy, blister-like rash along with fever and fatigue.

questions.explanation: Malaria is not a viral disease and is caused by the protozoan Plasmodium. All other options are viral infections that include AIDS caused by the HIV virus, influenza caused by the influenza virus, chickenpox caused by the varicella-zoster virus (VZV), and rabies caused by the rabies lyssavirus.

questions.text: How is HIV transmitted?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: HIV is transmitted by intimate sexual contact.
HIV can be spread through blood during blood transfusion, acupuncture, needles, etc.
HIV can be transmitted from the mother to the baby via breastfeeding.
HIV can be transmitted via the exchange of a variety of body fluids from infected people, such as blood, breast milk, semen, and vaginal secretions.

questions.explanation: HIV is a sexually transmitted disease that spreads to the blood, especially in target cells of the immune system known as T-cells; thus, the virus can also be transmitted through blood transfusions. Since the virus is very small in size, it can also pass from mother to child through breast milk. Hence, option D is the correct answer.

questions.text: Where does the AIDS virus infect?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: RBCs (red blood cells) carry oxygen throughout the body. They are not part of the body's immune system and do not play a role in fighting infection.
Platelets are responsible for blood clotting. They are not part of the body's immune system and do not play a role in fighting infection.

The human immunodeficiency virus (HIV) infects leukocytes, also known as white blood cells. These cells are part of the body's immune system and help fight infection. HIV targets a specific type of leukocyte called a CD4+ T cell. CD4+ T cells help the body fight infection by signaling other immune cells to attack the invading organism.
HIV mainly targets CD4 T helper cells because they have CD4 receptors. Red blood cells and platelets lack these receptors, so the virus cannot infect them.

questions.explanation: The human immunodeficiency virus (HIV) infects leukocytes, also known as white blood cells. These cells are part of the body's immune system and help fight infection. HIV targets a specific type of leukocyte called a CD4+ T cell. CD4+ T cells help the body fight infection by signalling other immune cells to attack the invading organism.

questions.text: A remarkable feature of the poxvirus:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Poxviruses are the largest viruses known to science. They can be up to 200 nanometers in diameter, which is much larger than most other viruses, which are typically around 20-30 nanometers in diameter.
The DNA genome and envelope are also correct, but they are not as remarkable as the size of poxviruses.
The envelope option is not correct because it is not as remarkable as the size of pox viruses.
The size of poxviruses is the most remarkable feature of these viruses.

questions.explanation: Poxviruses are the largest viruses known to science. They can be up to 200 nanometers in diameter, which is much larger than most other viruses, which are typically around 20-30 nanometers in diameter.

questions.text: _ is a common causative agent of genital herpes.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: HSV-1 is incorrect, although it can cause oral herpes (cold sores) and in some cases, it can also cause genital herpes.

Genital herpes is a common sexually transmitted infection caused by the herpes simplex virus (HSV-2). Sexual contact is the primary way that the virus spreads. After the initial infection, the virus lies dormant in your body and can reactivate several times a year
Both A and B are partially correct, as both HSV-1 and HSV-2 can cause genital herpes. However, as mentioned above, HSV-2 is the primary cause of genital herpes.
None of these is incorrect, as at least one of the options, i.e., HSV-2, is a causative agent of genital herpes.

questions.explanation: Genital herpes is a common sexually transmitted infection caused by the herpes simplex virus (HSV-2). Sexual contact is the primary way that the virus spreads. After the initial infection, the virus lies dormant in your body and can reactivate several times a year.

questions.text: The three stages of HIV infection are
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The three stages of HIV infection are:Acute infection: This is the initial stage of infection, typically lasting a few weeks to a few months. During this stage, the virus is replicating rapidly, and the person may experience flu-like symptoms, such as fever, fatigue, and swollen lymph nodes.Chronic infection: This is the stage of infection that follows acute infection. During this stage, the virus is still replicating, but the person's immune system can keep the virus under control. This means that the person may not experience any symptoms, or they may only experience mild symptoms.AIDS: AIDS is the final stage of HIV infection. This occurs when the virus has severely compromised the immune system, and the person is at a high risk of developing opportunistic infections.Therefore, the correct order of the stages of HIV infection is acute infection → chronic infection → AIDS.
This is not the correct order.
This is not the correct order.
This is not the correct order.

questions.explanation: HIV infection progresses through three main stages. The first stage is acute HIV infection, which usually occurs 2–4 weeks after exposure. During this period, the virus replicates rapidly, causing a very high viral load, and many people develop flu-like symptoms such as fever, rash, sore throat, fatigue, and swollen lymph nodes, although some may not notice any symptoms. Because of the high viral load, individuals are highly infectious at this stage. The second stage is chronic HIV infection, also called the clinical latency or asymptomatic stage. This stage can last for 10 years or longer without treatment, though it may progress more quickly in some people. During this time, the virus continues to replicate at lower levels while the immune system works to control it. Many people do not have symptoms or only experience mild ones, such as fatigue or recurrent minor infections. With effective antiretroviral therapy (ART), individuals can stay in this stage indefinitely and live a near-normal lifespan. The final stage is AIDS (Acquired Immunodeficiency Syndrome), which occurs when the immune system is severely damaged. This is diagnosed when the CD4 T-cell count falls below 200 cells/mm³ or when certain opportunistic infections or cancers develop. Symptoms in this stage may include chronic fever, weight loss, night sweats, persistent diarrhea, and frequent or severe infections. Without treatment, survival after reaching AIDS is typically around three years, but with ART, progression to this stage can often be prevented.

questions.text: AIDS is caused by which viruses?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: HIV causes aids.
Retroviruses cause diseases and illnesses such as AIDS, but AIDS specifically is caused by the retrovirus HIV.
The influenza virus causes influenza.
Paramyxoviruses cause mumps and measles.

questions.explanation: Option: A AIDS is caused by the human immunodeficiency virus (HIV). AIDS is the name used to describe potentially life-threatening infections and illnesses that happen when the immune system has been severely damaged by the HIV virus. Option C: The influenza virus causes influenza. Option D :Paramyxoviruses cause mumps and measles.

questions.text: HIV mainly attacks:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: HIV mainly attacks the CD4 site of T cells. T cells are a type of white blood cell that is important for the immune system. They help the body fight off infection by recognizing and destroying foreign invaders.

B cells are a type of white blood cell that produce antibodies. Antibodies help the body fight off infection by binding to foreign invaders and marking them for destruction. HIV does not directly attack B cells.
"White blood cells" is a general term for cells that fight infection. HIV does not directly attack white blood cells, but it does attack T cells, which are a type of white blood cell.
None of these is the correct answer. HIV attacks a specific site on T cells, the CD4 site.

questions.explanation: HIV mainly attacks the CD4 site of T cells. T cells are a type of white blood cell that is important for the immune system. They help the body fight off infection by recognizing and destroying foreign invaders.

questions.text: It is incorrect about the virus:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Viruses are acellular in nature, as they are not made up of cells.
Viruses contain DNA or RNA as their genetic material.
Viruses contain DNA or RNA as their genetic material.

Viruses do not replicate on their own because they are not living organisms and lack the metabolic machinery. In order for a virus to replicate, it needs to infect a host cell. The virus's genetic material is then injected into the host cell, and the host cell's machinery is used to replicate the virus's genome and produce new virus particles.

questions.explanation: Option D is correct. Viruses do not replicate on their own because they are not living organisms and lack the metabolic machinery. In order for a virus to replicate, it needs to infect a host cell. The virus's genetic material is then injected into the host cell, and the host cell's machinery is used to replicate the virus's genome and produce new virus particles. Viruses are acellular. This means that they are not made up of cells. They are simply pieces of genetic material (DNA or RNA) that are wrapped in a protein coat.

questions.text: It is not true about viruses:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: A capsid is the protein coat that surrounds the genome of a virus. It is made up of protein subunits called capsomeres.

options.text: Both DNA and RNA together as genome
options.explanation: Viruses can have either DNA or RNA as their genome, but they cannot have both.

Some viruses are enveloped, which means they have a lipid membrane surrounding their capsid. This membrane is made up of molecules from the host cell that the virus has hijacked. Some viruses are naked and do not have a lipid layer.
Viruses can infect a wide variety of organisms, including animals, plants, and bacteria. Many viruses that infect animals can also infect humans.

questions.explanation: Viruses cannot have both DNA and RNA as their genome because each type requires a completely different replication strategy and enzymes. DNA viruses depend on DNA polymerases, while RNA viruses use RNA-dependent RNA polymerases, and these systems are not compatible within a single genome. Carrying both would be inefficient and unstable, so viruses are classified strictly as either DNA or RNA viruses, never both.

questions.text: What is the viral envelope composed of?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The capsid is a protein shell that surrounds and protects the viral genetic material (DNA or RNA). It is made of repeating protein subunits called capsomeres, forming structured shapes like icosahedrons or helices. The capsid also helps the virus attach to and enter host cells.

The viral envelope contains glycoproteins, which are proteins with sugar molecules attached. These glycoproteins allow the virus to bind to host cell receptors and facilitate entry. They also help the virus evade the immune system by masking viral proteins.
Viral envelop is derived from host cell membrane which is made up of lipoprotein which is lipids and proteins and all.
Viral envelop is derived from host cell membrane which is made up of lipoprotein which is lipids and proteins and all.

questions.explanation: The viral envelope is a lipid-based layer that surrounds some viruses, derived from the host cell’s membrane during viral replication. It is not made entirely by the virus but is borrowed from the host, with embedded viral glycoproteins that protrude from its surface. These glycoproteins are crucial for the virus to recognize and attach to specific receptors on host cells, allowing entry and infection. The envelope also helps the virus evade the host immune system, as the host-derived lipids can mask viral components, making it less visible to immune defenses.

questions.text: What are the subunits of capsids?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Capsomeres are tiny subunits that make up the protein coat, referred to as the capsid. Capsomeres are known as capsid subunits, which are the outer protective covering of proteins that protect the virus's genetic material. TMV is also known as the tobacco mosaic virus. It is a type of single-stranded RNA virus.

Flagella are the organelles for bacterial locomotion. These supramolecular structures extend from the cytoplasm to the cell exterior and are composed of three major structural elements: the basal body, the hook, and the filament.

Hyphae are the feathery filaments that make up multicellular fungi. They release enzymes and absorb nutrients from a food source. Hyphae have a firm cell wall made of chitin. They grow from a tip and extend out in search of more food sources. Hyphae can also branch out and create a large network called a mycelium.
In biology, a septum (Latin for something that encloses; septa) is a wall dividing a cavity or structure into smaller ones.

questions.explanation: Capsomeres are tiny subunits that make up the protein coat, referred to as the capsid. Capsomeres are known as capsid subunits, which are the outer protective covering of proteins that protect the virus's genetic material. TMV is also known as the tobacco mosaic virus. It is a type of single-stranded RNA virus.

options.explanation: Herpesviruses also have an icosahedral capsid, but theirs is made of 162 capsomeres. These capsomeres form a strong, symmetric shell around the viral DNA. Compared to adenoviruses, the herpes capsid is larger and sits within an additional layer called the tegument, followed by the outer envelope.

This option is not correct for an adenovirus. The whole particle of adenovirus is about 70 to 100 nanometers across. The shell itself is built from 252 repeating protein subunits called capsomeres, which fit together like pieces of a geometric puzzle to form the protective coat around the DNA.

The adenovirus particle consists of an icosahedral protein shell surrounding a protein core that contains the linear, double-stranded DNA genome. The shell, which is 70 to 100 nm in diameter, is made up of 252 structural capsomeres.

This option is not correct for an adenovirus. The whole particle of adenovirus is about 70 to 100 nanometers across. The shell itself is built from 252 repeating protein subunits called capsomeres, which fit together like pieces of a geometric puzzle to form the protective coat around the DNA.

questions.explanation: The adenovirus particle consists of an icosahedral protein shell surrounding a protein core that contains the linear, double-stranded DNA genome. The shell, which is 70 to 100 nm in diameter, is made up of 252 structural capsomeres. 162 capsomeres are found in herpes virus. So, option C is correct.

questions.text: The complete, mature, and infectious particle is known as
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: A primary function of the capsid is to protect the viral genome from environmental conditions and ultimately to deliver the genome to the interior of a homologous host cell.

The virion, that is, the complete infectious virus particle, includes a genome comprising one or a few molecules of either DNA or RNA, surrounded by a morphologically defined protein coat, the capsid; the capsid and the enclosed nucleic acid together constitute the nucleocapsid.

Bacteriophages, also known as phages, are viruses that infect and replicate only in bacterial cells. They are ubiquitous in the environment and are recognized as the most abundant biological agent on earth. They are extremely diverse in size, morphology, and genomic organization.

A nucleus, as related to genomics, is the membrane-enclosed organelle within a cell that contains the chromosomes. An array of holes, or pores, in the nuclear membrane allows for the selective passage of certain molecules (such as proteins and nucleic acids) into and out of the nucleus.

questions.explanation: The virion, that is, the complete infectious virus particle, includes a genome comprising one or a few molecules of either DNA or RNA, surrounded by a morphologically defined protein coat, the capsid; the capsid and the enclosed nucleic acid together constitute the nucleocapsid.

Aspergillus is a fungus (Kingdom Fungi) in which cell wall is mainly made of chitin.
Funaria is a bryophyte (Kingdom Plantae) in which cell wall is cellulosic in nature.

questions.text: Viruses may not replicate in:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Viruses are obligate intracellular parasites, meaning they require a host cell's machinery to replicate and produce new viral particles. Blood plasma is the liquid component of blood that lacks any cells necessary for viral replication, making it impossible for viruses to reproduce in this medium.
Viruses can replicate in plant cells, as certain plant viruses have adapted specifically to utilize the cellular machinery of these cells for their reproduction. Therefore, this option is incorrect.

Certain viruses, such as the herpes simplex virus and human papillomavirus, can effectively replicate in skin cells. This indicates that skin cells can serve as hosts for viral replication, making this option incorrect.
Hepatocytes are liver cells that are specifically targeted by viruses like hepatitis B and C. These viruses utilize hepatocytes for replication, making this option incorrect as well.

questions.explanation: Viruses are obligate intracellular parasites, meaning they require a host cells machinery to replicate and produce new viral particies. Blood plasma is the liquid component of blood. that does not contain any blood cells, such as red blood cells. white blood cells, or platelets. Since blood plasma lacks the necessary host cells for viral reproduction, viruses cannot replicate in blood plasma.

questions.text: The capsid, the protective coat of a virus, is made up of _ subunits known as capsomeres.

The capsid is not made of RNA. It is made up of protein subunits called capsomeres. RNA is the genetic material of some viruses. It is enclosed in the capsid, but it is not part of the capsid itself. The RNA is responsible for encoding the proteins that make up the capsid. This option is incorrect.

The capsid is not made of DNA. DNA is the genetic material of some viruses. It is enclosed in the capsid, but it is not part of the capsid itself. The DNA is responsible for encoding the proteins that make up the capsid.

questions.explanation: Option B is correct. Chemically, a virion is made up of a core of genetic material: either DNA or RNA surrounded by a protective coat called a capsid, which is made up of protein. The capsid of viruses is made up of protein subunits known as capsomeres. The number of capsomeres is characteristic of a particular virus. The capsid is not made up of lipids, RNA and DNA.

questions.text: The filterable agents were first purified in 1935, when Stanley was successful in crystallizing the _
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Karl Landsteiner and Erwin Popper discovered the poliovirus in 1908. Landsteiner was an Austrian-American pathologist and immunologist, while Popper was an Austrian physician. They isolated the virus from the spinal cord of a child who had died of poliomyelitis.

Stanley was the first scientist to crystallize the tobacco mosaic virus in 1935. TMV is transmitted from infected plants to healthy plants through contact, such as by touching an infected leaf or by using the same tools on both infected and healthy plants. TMV can also be transmitted through the air, but this is less common.

Baruch Blumberg, who discovered the hepatitis B virus (HBV) in 1967. Blumberg was an Australian-American physician and immunologist who was awarded the Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine in 1976 for his discovery.

Wilson Smith, Christopher H. Andrewes, and Patrick P. Laidlaw are credited with the discovery of the influenza virus in 1933. They were working at the National Institute for Medical Research in London, England, when they isolated the virus from the throat washings of a patient with influenza.

questions.explanation: Option B is correct. The poliovirus was discovered in 1908 by Karl Landsteiner and Erwin Popper, who isolated it from the spinal cord of a child who died of poliomyelitis. In 1935, Wendell Stanley first crystallized the tobacco mosaic virus (TMV), which spreads mainly through contact between infected and healthy plants. The hepatitis B virus (HBV) was discovered by Baruch Blumberg in 1967, earning him the Nobel Prize in 1976. The influenza virus was identified in 1933 by Wilson Smith, Christopher H. Andrewes, and Patrick P. Laidlaw at the National Institute for Medical Research in London.

questions.text: Tobacco mosaic virus was discovered by:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Stanley was the first scientist to crystallise the tobacco mosaic virus in 1935.TMV is transmitted from infected plants to healthy plants through contact, such as by touching an infected leaf or by using the same tools on both infected and healthy plants. TMV can also be transmitted through the air, but this is less common.

Louis Pasteur was a French chemist and microbiologist who is considered the father of microbiology. He made many important discoveries in the field of microbiology, including the germ theory of disease, pasteurization, and vaccines against rabies and anthrax.

Ivanowsky was a Russian scientist who worked on the tobacco mosaic virus. He ground diseased leaves of tobacco plants and filtered them using a porcelain filter. He put this filtrate on healthy leaves and they got a disease. This showed tobacco plants were not infected by bacteria but by viruses.

Félix D'Hérelle was a French-Canadian microbiologist who is credited with the discovery of bacteriophages, viruses that infect bacteria. In 1917, he observed that certain bacterial cultures would clear up spontaneously, and he hypothesised that a virus was responsible for the clearing. He named the virus a bacteriophage, from the Greek words for "bacteria" and "eater."

questions.explanation: Stanley was the first scientist to crystallize the tobacco mosaic virus (TMV) in 1935. TMV spreads mainly through contact between infected and healthy plants or contaminated tools. Ivanowsky, a Russian scientist, discovered that TMV could pass through filters that trapped bacteria, proving it was caused by a virus. Félix D’Hérelle discovered bacteriophages, viruses that infect bacteria, in 1917. Louis Pasteur, the father of microbiology, developed the germ theory of disease and created vaccines for rabies and anthrax.

questions.text: Viruses cannot be grown on artificial culture media because they are:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: No, viruses are not facultative parasites. Facultative parasites are organisms that can live as either parasites or free-living organisms.
Endoparasites are organisms that live inside the cells of their hosts. Viruses are not able to live on their own. They need to infect a host cell to replicate.
Obligate intracellular parasites are organisms that can only live and reproduce inside the cells of a host organism. They are unable to live or reproduce on their own.
Ectoparasites are organisms that live on the outside of their hosts, and viruses can't be endoparasites, as they are dependent upon hosts for their survival.

questions.explanation: Option C is correct. Viruses cannot be grown in artificial media because they lack the metabolic machinery to synthesize their own nucleic acids and proteins. They are obligate intracellular parasites, meaning they can only live and reproduce inside host cells, unlike facultative parasites that can survive freely or endoparasitically, and ectoparasites that live on the surface of hosts.

questions.text: Among the following, _ enzyme is naturally found in HIV.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: They naturally have reverse transcriptase. DNA polymerase is an enzyme that plays a crucial role in DNA replication and repair. It is not naturally found in HIV.

They rely on the enzyme reverse transcriptase to transcribe their genome from RNA into DNA, which can then be integrated into the host's genome. This enzyme is naturally found in HIV. So, this option is correct.
They naturally have reverse transcriptase. RNA polymerase is an enzyme responsible for transcribing DNA into RNA during gene expression. It is not naturally found in HIV.
They naturally have reverse transcriptase. Ligase is an enzyme involved in the repair and joining of DNA strands. It is not naturally found in HIV.

questions.explanation: Among the options provided, "reverse transcriptase" is the enzyme naturally found in HIV.1. DNA polymerase: DNA polymerase is an enzyme that plays a crucial role in DNA replication and repair. It is not naturally found in HIV.2. Reverse transcriptase: Reverse transcriptase is an enzyme that is naturally found in HIV. It is responsible for the conversion of the virus's RNA genome into DNA, which can then be integrated into the host cell's genome. This process is a critical step in the HIV replication cycle.3. RNA polymerase: RNA polymerase is an enzyme responsible for transcribing DNA into RNA during gene expression. It is not naturally found in HIV.4. Ligase: Ligase is an enzyme involved in the repair and joining of DNA strands. It is not naturally found in HIV.So, among these options, only "reverse transcriptase" is the enzyme naturally found in HIV, and it is a key player in the virus's life cycle.

questions.text: Poliomyelitis affects:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Sensory neurons are responsible for carrying signals from sensory receptors, such as those in the skin or eyes, to the brain and spinal cord. These neurons allow us to feel sensations like touch, pain, and temperature. Poliomyelitis does not usually affect sensory neurons, which is why people with the disease can still feel sensations even in areas that are paralyzed.

Interneurons act as connectors between sensory and motor neurons within the central nervous system. They play a key role in processing information and coordinating reflexes. Poliomyelitis generally does not directly attack interneurons, although the overall communication between neurons can be affected when motor neurons are damaged.

Poliovirus replicates in the oropharynx and intestine and spreads through the blood to the nervous system. Poliovirus replicates in motor neurons located in the spinal cord. The death of these cells results in paralysis.

Autonomic neurons are part of the autonomic nervous system, which controls involuntary functions such as heart rate, digestion, and breathing. Poliomyelitis rarely affects these neurons, but in severe cases, the virus can impact the brainstem, which may disrupt breathing and other vital functions. This can make the disease life-threatening in advanced stages.

questions.explanation: Poliomyelitis mainly targets the motor neurons responsible for voluntary muscle movement, leading to muscle weakness and paralysis. Sensory neurons remain unaffected, so sensation is usually preserved. Interneurons and autonomic neurons are generally not directly attacked by the virus, though severe cases can sometimes interfere with vital functions if the brainstem is involved. Overall, the destruction of motor neurons is the key cause of the characteristic paralysis seen in polio.

questions.text: _ is known to cause cancers in animals.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The poliovirus primarily causes polio, a disease affecting the nervous system. It is not known for causing cancers in animals.
Chronic infection with the hepatitis B virus is associated with an increased risk of liver cancer (hepatocellular carcinoma). HBV is considered a carcinogenic virus.
HIV primarily causes immunodeficiency, leading to acquired immunodeficiency syndrome (AIDS). While it weakens the immune system, it is not a direct cause of cancer in animals.

Rous sarcoma virus is a retrovirus known to cause tumors in chickens, particularly sarcomas. It was the first virus identified as oncogenic (cancer-causing) and has provided insights into cancer research.

questions.explanation: Rous sarcoma virus causes cancer in animals because it carries an oncogene called the src gene that directly triggers uncontrolled cell division, leading to tumour formation. In contrast, poliovirus infects nerve cells and causes paralysis, not cancer. Hepatitis B virus can lead to liver cancer only after long-term chronic infection and inflammation, not through a direct oncogene. HIV, although a retrovirus like Rous sarcoma virus, does not cause cancer itself but weakens the immune system, allowing other cancer-causing viruses to act.

questions.text: AIDS is caused by:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: AIDS is not caused by bacteria. It is caused by a virus called the human immunodeficiency virus (HIV). HIV is a retrovirus, which means that it carries its genetic material in the form of RNA.

AIDS is caused by a virus. The virus is called the human immunodeficiency virus (HIV). HIV is a retrovirus, which means that it carries its genetic material in the form of RNA. The RNA is reverse transcribed into DNA by the virus's reverse transcriptase enzyme. The DNA is then integrated into the DNA of the host cell, where it can be replicated and transcribed into new viral RNA.

AIDS is not caused by fungi. Fungi are a different type of pathogen than viruses. Fungi are single-celled or multicellular organisms that can cause a variety of diseases, including athlete's foot, ringworm, and candidiasis. However, fungi do not cause AIDS.
AIDS is not caused by algae. Algae are a type of plant that can cause a variety of health problems, but they do not cause AIDS.

questions.explanation: Option B is correct. Yes, AIDS is caused by a virus. The virus is called the human immunodeficiency virus (HIV). HIV is a retrovirus, which means that it carries its genetic material in the form of RNA. The RNA is reverse transcribed into DNA by the virus's reverse transcriptase enzyme. The DNA is then integrated into the DNA of the host cell, where it can be replicated and transcribed into new viral RNA. It is not caused by bacteria, fungi, or algae.

questions.text: Viruses can infect and parasitize _.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Yes, viruses can infect and parasitise mammals. Viruses are capable of infecting a wide range of mammalian species, including humans, dogs, cats, cows, and many others.
Yes, viruses can infect and parasitise humans. Many viral diseases affect humans, such as the common cold, influenza, HIV/AIDS, COVID-19, and more.

Yes, viruses that infect bacteria are known as bacteriophages. Bacteriophages are viruses that can parasitise and replicate within bacterial cells, ultimately leading to the destruction of the bacteria.

Correct option. Viruses are incredibly diverse and can infect and parasitize not only mammals and humans but also other organisms like bacteria, plants, and even other viruses. They are versatile infectious agents capable of interacting with a wide range of hosts and cells.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is D) All of these, as viruses can infect and parasitize mammals, humans, bacteria, and various other hosts.

questions.text: Which one is not a living characteristic of the virus?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Mutation refers to changes in the genetic material (DNA or RNA) of an organism, including viruses. Viruses can undergo mutations over time, leading to the development of new strains or variants. However, mutation is not a living characteristic itself; rather, it is a process that occurs in living organisms, including viruses.

The genome of a virus refers to its genetic material, which can be either DNA or RNA. The genome contains all the information necessary for the virus to replicate and produce new viral particles. The viral genome is a critical component for the virus's functioning and is considered a living characteristic of the virus.

Replication is the process by which a virus makes copies of its genetic material and produces new viral particles. It is a fundamental aspect of the virus's life cycle and is considered a living characteristic of the virus.

Crystallization is the process of forming crystals, which is not a living characteristic. In the context of viruses, crystallization refers to the process of purifying and obtaining a virus in a crystal form for structural studies and research purposes. Crystals of viruses can be analyzed using X-ray crystallography to determine their three-dimensional structures. Therefore, this option is accurate

questions.explanation: Viruses exhibit certain living characteristics such as mutation, replication, and the presence of a genome, but not crystallization. Their ability to mutate allows them to evolve and adapt to changing environments, while replication, though dependent on a host cell, shows their capacity to reproduce like living organisms. The presence of genetic material (DNA or RNA) further emphasizes their biological nature, as it stores and transfers hereditary information. In contrast, crystallization is a physical property that occurs when viruses are isolated and purified, reflecting their non-living aspect rather than a life process. Thus, mutation, replication, and genome represent the living characteristics of viruses, whereas crystallization highlights their non-living nature.

questions.text: A characteristic of viruses is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Viruses lack the cellular machinery necessary for independent metabolism. Unlike living organisms, they cannot carry out metabolic processes such as energy production or nutrient utilisation on their own. They lack the necessary organelles and enzymes to perform these functions.
Viruses require living host cells to replicate and multiply. They cannot multiply outside of a living cell because they lack the cellular machinery needed for replication and protein synthesis.
As mentioned earlier, viruses do not possess independent metabolism. They rely on the metabolic processes of host cells to reproduce and carry out their life cycle.

options.text: It can be a culture in a cell-free medium
options.explanation: Viruses generally cannot be cultured in a cell-free medium like bacteria or other microorganisms. They require living cells to infect and replicate. While some advancements have been made in culturing certain viruses in cell-free systems, this is not a general characteristic of viruses.

questions.explanation: Viruses lack the cellular machinery necessary for independent metabolism. Unlike living organisms, they cannot carry out metabolic processes such as energy production or nutrient utilization on their own. They lack the necessary organelles and enzymes to perform these functions.

questions.text: Which of the following is a pair of viral diseases?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is correct because the common cold is caused by the common cold virus (rhinovirus), and AIDS is caused by HIV (Human Immunodeficiency Virus).
This option is incorrect because the common cold is caused by viruses, whereas dysentery can be caused by either bacteria or parasites, but not viruses.
This option is incorrect because typhoid is caused by Salmonella typhi. Tuberculosis is caused by Mycobacterium tuberculosis. Typhoid and tuberculosis are bacterial diseases, not viral.
This option is incorrect because AIDS is a viral disease caused by the HIV virus. On the other hand, ringworm is a fungal disease, not viral.

questions.explanation: The common cold is a viral disease. It is caused by rhinoviruses. It causes fever and pain all over the body and affects the nose, throat, and air passages. AIDS (acquired immunodeficiency syndrome) is a disorder of the cell-mediated immune system of the body. It is caused by HIV (human immunodeficiency virus). HIV is a retrovirus that attacks helper T cells.

questions.text: Infectious proteins are present in:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is incorrect because geminiviruses are a group of plant-infecting viruses characterized by their unique twin (geminate) particle structure. They do not consist of proteins alone, as viruses typically have a protein coat and genetic material.

This option is correct because prions are infectious agents made up of misfolded proteins. They can lead to neurodegenerative diseases by promoting the misfolding of normal proteins in the brain, making them unique among infectious agents.

This option is incorrect because viroids are the smallest known infectious agents and consist solely of short strands of circular, single-stranded RNA. They lack a protein coat and do not involve proteins in their structure.

This option is incorrect because satellite viruses are subviral agents that depend on a helper virus for replication. They do not consist solely of proteins, as they require a viral coat and cannot independently function.

questions.explanation: This option is correct because Prions are the infectious agents composed of abnormal misfolded proteins that have the ability to induce similar misfolding of normal proteins in the brain.

questions.text: Stanley crystallized:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: HIV is a virus that attacks the immune system, leading to acquired immunodeficiency syndrome (AIDS). While research and crystallography studies have been done on HIV, it was not specifically Stanley's experiment.
In 1935, Wendell Stanley successfully crystallized the tobacco mosaic virus (TMV). His work was groundbreaking and provided important insights into the nature of viruses.
The hepatitis B virus is a virus that causes liver infections. While it has been studied extensively, there is no specific information linking Stanley to the crystallization of HBV.
T2 is a bacteriophage, which is a virus that infects and replicates within bacteria. However, there is no specific information regarding Stanley's work with the T2 bacteriophage.

questions.explanation: In 1935, Wendell Meredith Stanley achieved a groundbreaking discovery by crystallizing the Tobacco Mosaic Virus (TMV). He purified the virus from infected tobacco leaves into a crystalline form that could be redissolved in water and still retain its infectious ability. This finding provided clear evidence that viruses were not typical living cells but rather entities with both chemical and biological properties. Stanley’s work demonstrated that viruses could be studied using chemical and physical methods, laying the foundation for modern virology and molecular biology. His achievement was so significant that he was awarded the Nobel Prize in Chemistry in 1946 for demonstrating the chemical nature of viruses through the crystallization of TMV.

questions.text: Demonstrate the correct use of articles and prepositions:_ man is mortal.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: The use of "a" indicates that the speaker is referring to one example of the noun, rather than a specific or previously mentioned instance of it (incorrect for this).
"An" is used when a noun or adjective that comes next begins with a vowel sound (incorrect for this)
"The" (before a singular or plural noun). It is an incorrect option according to rule no 1
The subject of the sentence is general. Therefore, Option D (no article) is correct.

questions.explanation: In the statement "Man is mortal," the absence of an article ("a" or "the") before "man" is due to the fact that "man" is used in a general or universal sense. When we talk about general truths, principles, or characteristics that apply universally, we often omit articles. This usage emphasizes the universality of the statement rather than a specific instance.

questions.text: Choose the correct sentence from the following:
questions.subject: English

options.text: The Return of the King which is a popular movie made a huge amount of money
options.explanation: The Return of the King is a title, therefore it must be in between quotation marks. Therefore, Option A is incorrect.

options.text: "The Return of the King", which is a popular movie made a huge amount of money.
options.explanation: Finally, 'which is a popular movie' is a dependent clause. Therefore, it must be contained within commas. This means option B is incorrect.

options.text: "The Return of the King", which is a popular movie, made a huge amount of money.
options.explanation: The Return of the King is a title, therefore it must be in between quotation marks. Therefore, Option A is incorrect. Since it is a title, it must also be capitalised. This means that option D is incorrect.Finally, 'which is a popular movie' is a dependent clause. Therefore, it must be contained within commas. This means option B is incorrect. Therefore, option C is the right answer.

options.text: "The return of the king", which is a popular, movie, made a huge amount of money.
options.explanation: Since it is a title, it must also be capitalised. This means that option D is incorrect.

questions.explanation: The Return of the King is a title, therefore it must be in between quotation marks. Therefore, Option A is incorrect. Since it is a title, it must also be capitalised. This means that option D is incorrect.Finally, 'which is a popular movie' is a dependent clause. Therefore, it must be contained within commas. This means option B is incorrect. Therefore, option C is the right answer.

questions.text: I advised her _ drink it.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: Don’t is used for present tense in a negative sense. As the given sentence is past tense, it is an incorrect option.
B is correct because it makes sense…When you want your writing to sound more formal and the sentence sounds better, you should use not to. Using not to protects the infinitive form of the verb.

Many grammarians are resolute that infinitives shouldn’t be split. They argue that the infinitive form is the true form of the verb and to add anything between “to” and the verb is wrong. There are some examples where this is correct.If we take the example, “not to be rude, but it’s your fault,” the word “not” is used before the infinitive “to be.” In this sentence, not to cannot be replaced with to not as it changes the meaning and the sentence no longer makes any sense.
Don’t is used for present tense in a negative sense. As the given sentence is past tense, it is an incorrect option.

questions.explanation: B is correct because it makes sense…When you want your writing to sound more formal and the sentence sounds better, you should use not to. Using not to protects the infinitive form of the verb. Many grammarians are resolute that infinitives shouldn’t be split. They argue that the infinitive form is the true form of the verb and to add anything between “to” and the verb is wrong. There are some examples where this is correct. If we take the example, “not to be rude, but it’s your fault,” the word “not” is used before the infinitive “to be.” In this sentence, not to cannot be replaced with to not as it changes the meaning and the sentence no longer makes any sense.

questions.text: Demonstrate the control of tenses and sentence structure:_ down with a pen and paper, I began to make a list of the ingredients I needed.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: Option A is wrong as it does not make any sense.

Sitting down is the correct answer…Here are some common ways we use present participle clauses. Note that present participles have a similar meaning to active verbs. To give the result of an action For example, The bomb exploded, destroying the building.To give the reason for an action For example, Knowing she loved reading, Richard bought her a book.To talk about an action that happened at the same time as another action For example, Standing in the queue, I realised I didn't have any money.To add information about the subject of the main clause For example, Starting in the new year, the new policy bans cars in the city centre.
This is also wrong as the second part of the sentence belongs to the past tense.
This is completely wrong as the second part of the sentence belongs to the past tense.

questions.explanation: Sitting down is the correct answer…Here are some common ways we use present participle clauses. Note that present participles have a similar meaning to active verbs. To give the result of an action For example, The bomb exploded, destroying the building.To give the reason for an action For example, Knowing she loved reading, Richard bought her a book.To talk about an action that happened at the same time as another action For example, Standing in the queue, I realised I didn't have any money.To add information about the subject of the main clause For example, Starting in the new year, the new policy bans cars in the city centre.

questions.text: Comprehend key vocabulary. The valley is known for its _ growth of vegetation.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: "Luxurious" is an adjective that means rich, elegant, or sumptuous, usually referring to something related to comfort, opulence, or indulgence. However, "luxurious growth of vegetation" does not make sense in the context of the sentence. "Luxurious" is not the correct word to describe the growth of vegetation.

"Luxury" is a noun that refers to a state of great comfort, elegance, or extravagance. It is not the correct word to describe the growth of vegetation. In this context, "luxury" is not suitable to fill the blank.

"Luxuriant" is an adjective that means abundant, lush, or growing in large quantities. This is the correct word to describe the growth of vegetation in the valley. "Luxuriant growth of vegetation" indicates that the vegetation in the valley is abundant and thriving.
"Luxuriously" is an adverb that describes an action done in a luxurious or extravagant manner. It is not the correct word to describe the growth of vegetation in the context of the sentence.

questions.explanation: "Luxuriant" is an adjective that means abundant, lush, or growing in large quantities. This is the correct word to describe the growth of vegetation in the valley. "Luxuriant growth of vegetation" indicates that the vegetation in the valley is abundant and thriving.

questions.text: Which of the following words will fill in the blank most appropriately?Diseases like diabetes are supposed to be taken seriously or they can be _.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: Incorrect. "Cursing" means using offensive language, which doesn’t fit the context of disease consequences.
"Healthy" is the opposite of what the sentence implies — diseases aren’t beneficial.
"Fatal" means causing death, which fits the warning tone of the sentence perfectly.
"Impersonating" means pretending to be someone else, irrelevant to the context of diseases.

questions.explanation: Diseases like diabetes are serious and can have severe consequences if not properly managed. The term "fatal" implies that the disease can potentially lead to death if not treated or controlled effectively.

questions.text: Fill in the blank with the correct word. The shepherd ploughed this mountain with cattle the first time it _ ever ploughed.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: The sentence is referring to a past event, indicating that the shepherd ploughed the mountain with cattle for the first time. The use of the past tense "was" is appropriate to describe the action that occurred in the past. Therefore, the correct sentence is: "The shephard ploughed this mountain with cattle for the first time it was ever ploughed."

The phrase "was been" is incorrect in this blank because it represents a redundant and grammatically incorrect use of verb forms."Was" and "been" are both past tense forms of the verb "to be," but they cannot be used together in a sentence. The correct forms are either "was" or "been," depending on the context.Here's a breakdown of the correct usage:"Was" is the simple past tense of the verb "to be" and is used when describing an action that happened in the past, without any specific relation to the present. For example: "He was happy yesterday.""Been" is the past participle of the verb "to be" and is used in combination with other verbs to indicate actions that started in the past and continue into the present or have completed at some point before the present. For example: "He has been to London."In the given sentence, "the first time it was ever ploughed," the use of "was" correctly indicates the past action of the mountain being ploughed for the first time. Adding "been" after "was" would create a grammatically incorrect structure, as "been" is not required in this context.Corrected sentence: The shepherd ploughed this mountain with cattle the first time it was ever ploughed.

The word "had" is incorrect in this blank because it represents the past perfect tense, which is not appropriate for this context.The past perfect tense is used to describe an action that occurred before another action in the past. It is formed by combining the past tense of "have" (had) with the past participle of the main verb. For example: "He had already eaten before I arrived."In the original sentence, we are not describing an action that occurred before another past action. Instead, we are simply stating that the mountain was ploughed for the first time. There is no need to introduce a past perfect construction here because there is no other past action in the sentence that the ploughing happened before.

The phrase "had been" is the past perfect continuous tense, which is used to describe an action that started in the past, continued for some time, and then stopped before another past event. It is formed by combining the past perfect tense of "have" (had) with the past participle of the main verb (in this case, "been").

questions.explanation: The sentence is referring to a past event, indicating that the shepherd ploughed the mountain with cattle for the first time. The use of the past tense "was" is appropriate to describe the action that occurred in the past. Therefore, the correct sentence is: "The shephard ploughed this mountain with cattle for the first time it was ever ploughed."

questions.text: I. The literacy rate in the district has been increasing. II. The district administration has conducted extensive training program for the workers involved in the literacy drive.
questions.subject: Logical Reasoning

options.text: Statement I is the cause and statement II is its effect
Statement II is the cause and statement I is its effect
Both the statements I and II are independent causes
Both the statements I and II are effects of independent cause

questions.explanation: Statement 2 is the cause and statement 1 is its effect. The statement implies that the district administration conducted an extensive training program for the workers involved in the literacy drive (cause), which resulted in an increase in the literacy rate in the district (effect).

questions.text: Read the following statement, assuming everything in it to be true. Then decide which of the given suggested courses of action logically follow and are worth pursuing.Statement:'"Aalia wants to sleep but cannot due to regular noise in and around her house everyday."Courses of Action:I. Insert good quality noise blockers into her ears.II, Take strong sleeping pills."
questions.subject: Logical Reasoning
questions.explanation: Good noise blockers would help her fix the problem. Therefore, only statement I is correct.

questions.text: Some bags are pouches. All pouches are cases. No cases are purses. Which of the following conclusions are NECESSARILY TRUE?CONCLUSIONS:I. Some pouches are purses.II. Some bags are cases.III. No bags are purses.
questions.subject: Logical Reasoning
questions.explanation: I: Since all pouches are cases and no cases are purses then no pouches are purses. This statement is false. II: Since some bags are pouches and all pouches are cases, this indicates that some bags(that are pouches) can also be cases. This statement is also true. III: This statement can be false because the bags that are not pouches can be purses.
